title: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections
authors: Peter F. Weller, Edward T. Ryan

items.subtopic: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections | TRICHINELLOSIS (part 1)
items.title: Epidemiology of Trichinellosis
items.content: Trichinellosis is caused by ingestion of meat containing cysts of various species of Trichinella, primarily from carnivorous or omnivorous animals. The disease has a global distribution with different species prevalent in specific regions.
keyPoints: At least nine species and 13 genotypes of Trichinella infect humans.

T. spiralis is widespread; T. nativa found in Arctic/subarctic regions; T. nelsoni in equatorial Africa; T. britovi in Europe, western Africa, and Asia; T. murrelli in North America and Japan; T. papua in Papua New Guinea, Thailand, Taiwan, Cambodia; T. zimbabwensis in Tanzania; T. patagoniensis in South American cougars.
Historically common from pork consumption but now also from other meats like bear, walrus, dogs, horses, and contaminated beef or horse meat.
Annual cases in the U.S. have decreased to 10-20 due to control measures.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections | TRICHINELLOSIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections | TRICHINELLOSIS (part 1)
items.title: Pathogenesis of Trichinellosis
items.content: The pathogenesis involves ingestion, enteric invasion by larvae, larval migration to muscle tissue, and subsequent encystment. The clinical manifestations are linked to these phases.
keyPoints: Encysted larvae liberated in the small bowel; mature into adults within ~1 week.
Newborn larvae migrate via circulation to striated muscles except for T. pseudospiralis, T. papuae, and T. zimbabwensis which do not encyst.
Host immune response expels intestinal worms but has limited impact on muscle-dwelling larvae.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections | TRICHINELLOSIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections | TRICHINELLOSIS (part 1)
items.title: Clinical Features of Trichinellosis
items.content: The clinical features vary depending on the severity of infection, with light infections often asymptomatic and heavy infections potentially life-threatening.
keyPoints: Light infections (<10 larvae/g muscle) are usually asymptomatic; heavy infections (>50 larvae/g muscle) can be severe or fatal.
Enteric phase symptoms: diarrhea, abdominal pain, constipation, nausea within first weeks.
Second week: fever, eosinophilia, periorbital and facial edema, subconjunctival and retinal hemorrhages, maculopapular rash, headache, cough, dyspnea, dysphagia.
Larval encystment in muscles leads to myositis with myalgias, muscle edema, weakness; involves extraocular, biceps, jaw, neck, lower back, diaphragm muscles.
Symptoms peak ~3 weeks post-infection and subside gradually during convalescence.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections | TRICHINELLOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosis of Trichinellosis: Laboratory Findings and Clinical Correlation
items.content: In symptomatic trichinellosis, blood eosinophilia develops in over 90% of patients, peaking at levels greater than 50% two to four weeks post-infection. Serum muscle enzymes such as creatine phosphokinase are also elevated. A presumptive clinical diagnosis can be made based on symptoms like fevers, eosinophilia, periorbital edema, and myalgias following the consumption of suspect meat. Confirmation is achieved by a rise in parasite-specific antibody titers after three weeks or through surgical muscle biopsy.
keyPoints: Blood eosinophilia develops in >90% of symptomatic patients, peaking at >50% 2-4 weeks post-infection.
Serum creatine phosphokinase levels are elevated in most symptomatic cases.
Presumptive diagnosis based on fevers, eosinophilia, periorbital edema, and myalgias after suspect meal consumption.
Parasite-specific antibody titer rise confirms diagnosis post third week of infection.
Definitive diagnosis via surgical biopsy of at least 1 g of involved muscle; examination with fresh tissue compression between glass slides.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · TRICHINELLOSIS (part 2) | Trichinellosis

items.title: Management and Prevention Strategies for Trichinellosis
items.content: Lightly infected trichinellosis patients typically recover with bed rest, anti-pyretics, and analgesics. Severe cases involving myositis or myocarditis benefit from glucocorticoids like prednisone. Mebendazole and albendazole are effective against the parasite's enteric stages but not conclusively against encysted larvae. Preventive measures include cooking pork until no longer pink or freezing at -15°C for three weeks, though Arctic T. nativa larvae in walrus or bear meat may resist these methods.
keyPoints: Lightly infected patients recover with bed rest, anti-pyretics, and analgesics.
Glucocorticoids (e.g., prednisone) are beneficial for severe myositis and myocarditis.
Mebendazole and albendazole target enteric stages; efficacy against encysted larvae is not conclusive.
Prevention: Cook pork until no longer pink or freeze at -15°C for 3 weeks.
Arctic T. nativa larvae in walrus/bear meat may resist freezing.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · TRICHINELLOSIS (part 2) | Trichinellosis

items.title: Visceral Larva Migrans: Recognizing and Managing Systemic Involvement
items.content: Visceral larva migrans is caused by nematode larvae, primarily $Toxocara	ext{ }canis$, migrating through human tissues. This results in eosinophilic inflammation without adult worm development. The syndrome often affects preschool children and presents with symptoms like fever, malaise, anorexia, weight loss, cough, wheezing, rashes, and hepatosplenomegaly. Peripheral eosinophilia can reach levels up to 90%. Diagnosis involves recognizing these clinical features along with laboratory findings such as eosinophilia, leukocytosis, and hypergammaglobulinemia.
keyPoints: Most common form: toxicariasis due to $Toxocara	ext{ }canis$ larvae.
Symptoms include fever, malaise, anorexia, weight loss, cough, wheezing, rashes, hepatosplenomegaly.
Peripheral eosinophilia may approach 90%.
Diagnosis confirmed by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay for toxocara antibodies; stool examination is not useful.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · VISCERAL AND OCULAR LARVA MIGRANS

items.title: Ocular Larva Migrans: Identifying Ophthalmic Manifestations
items.content: The ocular form of larva migrans occurs when Toxocara larvae invade the eye, forming an eosinophilic granulomatous mass. This can mimic retinoblastoma and lead to unnecessary enucleation if misdiagnosed. Symptoms include unilateral visual disturbances, strabismus, and eye pain. Unlike visceral larva migrans, ocular involvement typically occurs in older children or young adults without eosinophilia or systemic symptoms.
keyPoints: Ocular form involves an eosinophilic granulomatous mass in the retina.
Can mimic retinoblastoma; careful diagnosis needed to avoid unnecessary enucleation.
Symptoms: unilateral visual disturbances, strabismus, eye pain.
Occurs more commonly in older children or young adults without systemic symptoms.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · VISCERAL AND OCULAR LARVA MIGRANS

items.title: Diagnostic Approach to Larva Migrans Syndromes
items.content: Diagnosis of visceral larva migrans involves recognizing clinical features such as eosinophilia, leukocytosis, and hypergammaglobulinemia. Chest x-rays may show transient pulmonary infiltrates in patients with pneumonitis symptoms. Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay for toxocara antibodies can confirm the diagnosis, though it may be negative in ocular cases. Stool examination is not useful as larvae do not develop into egg-producing adults in humans.
keyPoints: Eosinophilia, leukocytosis, and hypergammaglobulinemia are diagnostic indicators for visceral form.
Chest x-rays may show pulmonary infiltrates in pneumonitis cases.
Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay confirms diagnosis; may be negative in ocular cases.
Stool examination is not useful as larvae do not produce eggs in humans.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · VISCERAL AND OCULAR LARVA MIGRANS

items.title: Management of Severe Visceral Larva Migrans Involvement
items.content: In cases of severe myocardial, CNS, or pulmonary involvement due to visceral larva migrans, glucocorticoids are recommended to reduce inflammatory complications. Although anthelmintic drugs like albendazole have not been conclusively proven to alter the disease course, they are often used alongside steroids for moderate to severe cases.
keyPoints: Glucocorticoids may be employed in severe myocardial, CNS, or pulmonary involvement.
Albendazole is commonly used with concomitant steroids despite lack of conclusive evidence altering disease course.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Visceral and Ocular Larva Migrans | CUTANEOUS LARVA MIGRANS | ANGIOSTRONGYLIASIS

items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Cutaneous Larva Migrans
items.content: Cutaneous larva migrans, caused by Ancylostoma braziliense larvae, presents as serpiginous skin eruptions. Diagnosis is clinical, with symptoms alleviated by ivermectin or albendazole. The condition typically resolves on its own within weeks to months.
keyPoints: Clinical diagnosis based on characteristic erythematous lesions.
Symptoms can be treated with ivermectin or albendazole.
Lesions resolve without treatment in a few weeks to months.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Visceral and Ocular Larva Migrans | CUTANEOUS LARVA MIGRANS | ANGIOSTRONGYLIASIS

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Diagnosis of Angiostrongyliasis
items.content: Angiostrongylus cantonensis causes eosinophilic meningitis, presenting with headaches, neck stiffness, nausea, vomiting, and paresthesias. CSF examination reveals elevated opening pressure, white blood cell count (150–2000/μL), eosinophilic pleocytosis (>20%), and elevated protein concentration.
keyPoints: Clinical presentation includes headache, neck stiffness, nausea, vomiting, and paresthesias.
CSF findings: Elevated opening pressure, WBC 150-2000/μL, eosinophilic pleocytosis >20%, elevated protein.
Diagnosis based on clinical presentation and CSF analysis.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Visceral and Ocular Larva Migrans | CUTANEOUS LARVA MIGRANS | ANGIOSTRONGYLIASIS

items.title: Management of Eosinophilic Meningitis due to Angiostrongyliasis
items.content: Eosinophilic meningitis from angiostrongyliasis often improves with supportive therapy. Steroids are crucial for reducing inflammation and should precede any specific chemotherapy like albendazole to prevent exacerbation of inflammatory brain lesions. Repeated lumbar punctures can aid in symptom relief, as the condition is typically self-limiting.
keyPoints: Supportive therapy often leads to improvement.
Steroids are essential before specific chemotherapy to reduce inflammation.
Albendazole may be used if steroids are administered first.
Repeated lumbar punctures can assist with symptom relief.
Infection is self-limited, and recovery is usually complete.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Angiostrongyliasis | GNATHOSTOMIASIS

items.title: Prevention of Angiostrongyliasis
items.content: Preventing angiostrongyliasis involves adequately cooking snails, crabs, and prawns, and inspecting vegetables for mollusk infestation. This is crucial as the infection can be acquired through ingestion of contaminated food.
keyPoints: Adequate cooking of snails, crabs, and prawns prevents infection.
Inspect vegetables for mollusk infestation to avoid transmission.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Angiostrongyliasis | GNATHOSTOMIASIS

items.subtopic: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections | TRICHINELLOSIS (part 1)
items.question: Which species of Trichinella is found in Arctic and subarctic regions?
items.explanation: T. nativa is present in Arctic and subarctic regions.

items.subtopic: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections | TRICHINELLOSIS (part 1)
items.question: What clinical feature is commonly associated with the larval migration phase of trichinellosis?
items.explanation: Periorbital and facial edema is common during the larval migration phase.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections | TRICHINELLOSIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections | TRICHINELLOSIS (part 1)
items.question: Which animal meat has increasingly been associated with human trichinellosis in North America?
items.explanation: Consumption of wild game, especially bear and walrus meat, accounts for the majority of cases reported each year in North America.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections | TRICHINELLOSIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections | TRICHINELLOSIS (part 1)
items.question: What is a common symptom during the muscle encystment phase of trichinellosis?
items.explanation: Symptoms of myositis with myalgias develop upon onset of larval encystment in muscle.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections | TRICHINELLOSIS (part 1)

items.question: Which laboratory finding is most commonly observed in patients with symptomatic trichinellosis?
options: Blood eosinophilia develops in >90% of patients.
Serum levels of liver enzymes are elevated in most symptomatic patients.
A rise in the titer of parasite-specific antibody occurs immediately after infection.
Muscle biopsy is required for all symptomatic patients.
items.explanation: Blood eosinophilia develops in >90% of patients with symptomatic trichinellosis and may peak at a level of >50% 2-4 weeks after infection.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · TRICHINELLOSIS (part 2) | Trichinellosis

items.question: What is the recommended method for examining fresh muscle tissue to diagnose trichinellosis?
options: Compressing between glass slides and microscopic examination.
Culturing on a nutrient agar plate.
items.explanation: The fresh muscle tissue should be compressed between glass slides and examined microscopically because larvae may be missed by examination of routine histopathologic sections alone.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · TRICHINELLOSIS (part 2) | Trichinellosis

items.question: Which treatment is beneficial for severe myositis and myocarditis in trichinellosis?
items.explanation: Glucocorticoids like prednisone are beneficial for severe myositis and myocarditis in trichinellosis.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · TRICHINELLOSIS (part 2) | Trichinellosis

items.question: Which nematode is most commonly responsible for causing visceral larva migrans in humans?
items.explanation: Toxocara canis is the most common cause of visceral larva migrans in humans.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · VISCERAL AND OCULAR LARVA MIGRANS

items.question: How do humans primarily acquire toxocariasis?
options: Through direct contact with infected dogs or cats
By ingesting soil/sand contaminated by dog or cat feces containing infective eggs
Via transplacental transmission from mother to child
items.explanation: Humans primarily acquire toxocariasis by ingesting soil/sand contaminated with infective Toxocara canis eggs.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · VISCERAL AND OCULAR LARVA MIGRANS

items.question: What is a common clinical feature of visceral larva migrans?
items.explanation: A common feature of visceral larva migrans is extraordinary peripheral eosinophilia, which may approach 90%.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · VISCERAL AND OCULAR LARVA MIGRANS

items.question: Which diagnostic test can confirm the clinical diagnosis of toxocariasis?
options: Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) for toxocara antibodies
items.explanation: The clinical diagnosis of toxocariasis can be confirmed by an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) for toxocara antibodies.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · VISCERAL AND OCULAR LARVA MIGRANS

items.subtopic: Visceral and Ocular Larva Migrans | CUTANEOUS LARVA MIGRANS | ANGIOSTRONGYLIASIS
items.question: Which anthelmintic drug is commonly used in conjunction with steroids to treat severe visceral larva migrans?
items.explanation: Most individuals with moderate to severe visceral larva migrants receive albendazole with concomitant steroids.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Visceral and Ocular Larva Migrans | CUTANEOUS LARVA MIGRANS | ANGIOSTRONGYLIASIS

items.subtopic: Visceral and Ocular Larva Migrans | CUTANEOUS LARVA MIGRANS | ANGIOSTRONGYLIASIS
items.question: What is the primary cause of cutaneous larva migrans?
items.explanation: Cutaneous larva migrans is caused by the burrowing larvae of animal hookworms, usually Ancylostoma braziliense.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Visceral and Ocular Larva Migrans | CUTANEOUS LARVA MIGRANS | ANGIOSTRONGYLIASIS

items.subtopic: Visceral and Ocular Larva Migrans | CUTANEOUS LARVA MIGRANS | ANGIOSTRONGYLIASIS
items.question: Which of the following is a recommended control measure for preventing Toxocara infections?
options: Prohibiting dog excreta in public parks and playgrounds
items.explanation: Control measures include prohibiting dog excreta in public parks and playgrounds.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Visceral and Ocular Larva Migrans | CUTANEOUS LARVA MIGRANS | ANGIOSTRONGYLIASIS

items.subtopic: Visceral and Ocular Larva Migrans | CUTANEOUS LARVA MIGRANS | ANGIOSTRONGYLIASIS
items.question: What is the typical clinical presentation of eosinophilic meningitis caused by Angiostrongylus cantonensis?
options: Excruciating frontal, occipital, or bitemporal headache
Rapid onset of high fever and chills
items.explanation: Patients usually present with an excruciating frontal, occipital, or bitemporal headache.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Visceral and Ocular Larva Migrans | CUTANEOUS LARVA MIGRANS | ANGIOSTRONGYLIASIS

items.question: Which treatment is considered of uncertain benefit and should not be administered without steroids in cases of cerebral angiostrongyliasis?
items.explanation: Specific chemotherapy is of uncertain benefit and should not be given without steroids.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Angiostrongyliasis | GNATHOSTOMIASIS

items.question: What is the most common method of diagnosis for A. costaricensis infection?
items.explanation: Diagnosis of A. costaricensis is most commonly made at the time of surgical excision.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Angiostrongyliasis | GNATHOSTOMIASIS

items.question: Which preventive measure can help avoid angiostrongyliasis?
options: Adequately cooking snails, crabs, and prawns
items.explanation: The infection may be prevented by adequately cooking snails, crabs, and prawns.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Angiostrongyliasis | GNATHOSTOMIASIS

items.subtopic: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections | TRICHINELLOSIS (part 1)
items.statement: T. pseudospiralis larvae encapsulate in muscles.
items.explanation: Infections with T. pseudospiralis elicit a prolonged polymyositis-like illness because its larvae do not encapsulate in muscles.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections | TRICHINELLOSIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections | TRICHINELLOSIS (part 1)
items.statement: Most cases of trichinellosis are now acquired from the consumption of pork.
items.explanation: The majority of reported cases in North America result from the consumption of wild game, especially bear and walrus meat.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections | TRICHINELLOSIS (part 1)

items.statement: A rise in the titer of parasite-specific antibody usually occurs before the third week of infection.
items.explanation: A rise in the titer of parasite-specific antibody, which usually does not occur until after the third week of infection.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · TRICHINELLOSIS (part 2) | Trichinellosis

items.statement: Arctic T. nativa larvae can remain viable despite freezing at -15°C for 3 weeks.
items.explanation: Arctic T. nativa larvae in walrus or bear meat are relatively resistant and may remain viable despite freezing.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · TRICHINELLOSIS (part 2) | Trichinellosis

items.statement: Stool examination is useful in diagnosing toxocariasis.
items.explanation: Stool examination for parasite eggs is worthless in toxocariasis because the larvae do not develop into egg-producing adults in humans.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · VISCERAL AND OCULAR LARVA MIGRANS

items.statement: Ocular larva migrans commonly presents with eosinophilia.
items.explanation: Patients with ocular toxicariasis seldom have eosinophilia or visceral manifestations.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · VISCERAL AND OCULAR LARVA MIGRANS

items.subtopic: Visceral and Ocular Larva Migrans | CUTANEOUS LARVA MIGRANS | ANGIOSTRONGYLIASIS
items.statement: The larvae of Angiostrongylus cantonensis are often seen in the cerebrospinal fluid during examination.
items.explanation: The larvae of A. cantonensis are only rarely seen in CSF.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Visceral and Ocular Larva Migrans | CUTANEOUS LARVA MIGRANS | ANGIOSTRONGYLIASIS

items.statement: Cutaneous larva migrans lesions typically resolve without treatment.
items.explanation: The animal hookworm larvae do not mature in humans and will die after an interval, with resolution of skin lesions.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Visceral and Ocular Larva Migrans | CUTANEOUS LARVA MIGRANS | ANGIOSTRONGYLIASIS

items.statement: Infection with A. costaricensis is primarily acquired through ingestion of contaminated water.
items.explanation: A. costaricensis infection occurs following ingestion of slugs or raw produce contaminated by slug slime, not directly from water.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Angiostrongyliasis | GNATHOSTOMIASIS

items.statement: Gnathostoma spinigerum can cause eosinophilic meningoencephalitis in humans.
items.explanation: Infection with Gnathostoma spinigerum larvae can indeed lead to eosinophilic meningoencephalitis.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Angiostrongyliasis | GNATHOSTOMIASIS

items.subtopic: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections | TRICHINELLOSIS (part 1)
items.assertion: Human trichinellosis can be acquired from the ingestion of contaminated beef.
items.reason: Beef and horse meat have been implicated in outbreaks when contaminated or adulterated with trichinous pork.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections | TRICHINELLOSIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections | TRICHINELLOSIS (part 1)
items.assertion: T. spiralis is found only in domestic swine.
items.reason: T. spiralis is distributed worldwide among a variety of carnivorous and omnivorous animals.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because T. spiralis is not limited to domestic swine; the reason is true.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections | TRICHINELLOSIS (part 1)

items.assertion: Mebendazole and albendazole are effective against encysting/encysted larvae of T. spiralis.
items.reason: Their efficacy against enteric stages of the parasite has not been conclusively demonstrated.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because their efficacy against encysting/encysted larvae has not been conclusively demonstrated, but they are active against enteric stages.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · TRICHINELLOSIS (part 2) | Trichinellosis

items.assertion: Visceral larva migrans can lead to hepatosplenomegaly.
items.reason: The syndrome involves intense local eosinophilic granulomatous responses in various organs including the liver and spleen.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · VISCERAL AND OCULAR LARVA MIGRANS

items.assertion: Ocular larva migrans is more common in preschool children.
items.reason: The ocular form develops in older children or young adults with no history of pica.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true; ocular larva migrans typically occurs in older children or young adults.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · VISCERAL AND OCULAR LARVA MIGRANS

items.subtopic: Visceral and Ocular Larva Migrans | CUTANEOUS LARVA MIGRANS | ANGIOSTRONGYLIASIS
items.assertion: The diagnosis of cutaneous larva migrans is often made on clinical grounds.
items.reason: Skin biopsies only rarely detect diagnostic larvae.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the diagnosis is typically based on clinical presentation.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Visceral and Ocular Larva Migrans | CUTANEOUS LARVA MIGRANS | ANGIOSTRONGYLIASIS

items.subtopic: Visceral and Ocular Larva Migrans | CUTANEOUS LARVA MIGRANS | ANGIOSTRONGYLIASIS
items.assertion: Serologic tests for Angiostrongylus cantonensis are always positive at the onset of clinical symptoms.
items.reason: These tests have not been standardized and may be negative at onset.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because serologic tests may be negative at onset; however, the reason is true.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Visceral and Ocular Larva Migrans | CUTANEOUS LARVA MIGRANS | ANGIOSTRONGYLIASIS

items.assertion: Supportive therapy alone is often sufficient for recovery in many individuals with cerebral angiostrongyliasis.
items.reason: Cerebral angiostrongyliasis has a self-limited course and most patients recover completely.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why supportive therapy alone is often sufficient.
items.reference: Trichinellosis and Other Tissue Nematode Infections · Angiostrongyliasis | GNATHOSTOMIASIS
